frameworks.name: model-based reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: planning over a causal world model to compute action values

frameworks.name: model-free reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: cached cheap action values from trial-and-error look-up

frameworks.name: dual-system decision making
frameworks.one_line: fast automatic vs slow deliberative control as competing systems

frameworks.name: accuracy-demand trade-off arbitration
frameworks.one_line: task redesigned to make the trade-off actually govern strategy choice

core_question: Do canonical two-step tasks actually embody an accuracy-versus-computation trade-off between model-free and model-based control?
key_assumptions: arbitration between strategies depends on an accuracy-demand trade-off
model-based control is more accurate but cognitively costlier
model-free control relies on cached, trial-and-error values
manipulations: task structure variants of the standard two-step paradigm
five factors that erode model-based effectiveness
a redesigned task that enforces an accuracy-demand trade-off
key_findings: the hallmark two-step task and variants do not impose an accuracy-demand trade-off
five identified factors reduce model-based estimation accuracy and choice impact
the redesigned task yields a genuine accuracy-demand trade-off
humans spontaneously increase model-based reliance on the redesigned task
interpretations: standard paradigms underestimate when model-based control pays off
arbitration toward model-based control emerges when accuracy gains matter
